title: Genesis 24
subtitle: The Marriage of Isaac and Rebekah

sections.header: Verses 1–9 — Abraham's Commission: A Wife for Isaac

poi.name: Aram Naharaim / Nahor's city
poi.coords: Upper Mesopotamia; modern SE Turkey/NW Syria
poi.text: The servant is sent to "my country and my own relatives" — Aram Naharaim ("Aram of the two rivers"), the region around Harran. Nahor's city is the family's Mesopotamian base. The servant's journey is a geographical reversal of Abram's original call: back to Mesopotamia to bring the covenant forward.

notes.note: MacArthur: Abraham's instruction to his servant -- swear by the LORD God of heaven and earth that you will not get a wife for my son from the Canaanites but will go to my own relatives -- reflects the covenant principle of separation. The wife of the covenant heir must come from the covenant line, not from the cursed Canaanite culture.

MacArthur: Abraham's confidence -- 'He will send his angel before you so that you can get a wife for my son from there' -- is not presumption but covenant faith. The same God who called him from Ur, who gave him Isaac, who provided the ram, will guide the servant to the right woman. The covenant God governs the details of covenant succession.

heb.paragraph: Abraham "sent his servant" (šālaḥ, v.2) to find a wife for Isaac — the same verb used of God "sending" his covenant agents throughout Scripture. The servant is unnamed (possibly Eliezer of Damascus, mentioned in 15:2) but given a solemn oath-commission. The entire chapter is the longest narrative in Genesis, with the servant's story told twice — once as it happened, once as he recounts it. The doubling is theologically significant: covenant mission requires testimony, and testimony requires witness.

"Put your hand under my thigh" (v.2) — the oath-gesture of placing the hand under the patriarch's thigh (near the circumcision/covenant-organ) signals the solemnity of the commission. The same gesture appears at Jacob's deathbed (47:29). The thigh-oath invokes the covenant sign of circumcision: the servant is bound by the covenant itself, not merely by social obligation. Breaking this oath would be a breach of covenant-oath — one of the most serious offences in the ancient Near East.

"He will send his angel before you" (v.7) — Abraham's confidence that God will send a malʾāk to guide the servant echoes the angel who guided Hagar (16:7) and will guide Israel in the wilderness (Exod 23:20). The conviction that divine guidance accompanies covenant mission is not optimism but theology: the covenant God is the one who sends, sustains, and guides those sent on his behalf. The servant's subsequent prayer (vv.12-14) and its immediate answer (vv.15-27) confirm that the angel's guidance is real and specific.

hist.context: Abraham's insistence that Isaac not marry a Canaanite woman is the first explicit concern for covenant continuity in the next generation. The oath sworn on the patriarch's thigh (an ancient oath gesture, possibly relating to the "seed" — descendants) is the most solemn form of commitment in the ANE. The concern for endogamy (marriage within the kinship group) preserves the covenant line and echoes forward to the Mosaic prohibition of intermarriage with Canaanites (Deut 7:3).

refs.note: Isaac's sons will face the same issue: Esau's Canaanite wives "make life bitter" for Isaac and Rebekah; Jacob is specifically sent to Paddan-aram to find a wife from the family line.
Paul's "do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers" echoes the same theological principle — covenant integrity requires covenant partnership.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The marriage commission is the longest chapter in Genesis and the most elaborate narrative in the Abraham cycle. Sarna: "The servant's commission is given with extraordinary solemnity: the oath taken with the hand under the thigh (the reproductive organs), the double prohibition (no wife from Canaan, Isaac must not go back to Mesopotamia), and the servant's careful request for divine confirmation. The entire enterprise is structured as a test of divine providence."

Abraham's statement of faith — "the LORD will send his angel before you" — is a declaration of confidence in divine guidance. Sarna: "Abraham, who began his journey to an unknown land (12:1), now speaks with complete confidence about the divine guidance of a mission to the same region he left. The faith that was tested in the Akedah expresses itself in confident delegation."

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The oath under the thigh. Alter: "The gesture is unique to this passage and to 47:29 (Jacob and Joseph). Its connection to the reproductive organs suggests an oath sworn on the power of procreation — swearing by the very faculty through which the covenant promise is transmitted. It is the most intimate and physically specific oath in the Bible."

The servant's question and Abraham's answer establish the double constraint: no returning to Mesopotamia with Isaac, and no forcing the woman. Alter notes these two constraints shape the narrative throughout: the servant must find a willing woman who will come to a land she has never seen.

Calvin on the divine guidance of the servant: "Abraham declares that God will send his angel before the servant as a pledge of his own faith, to give the servant confidence. This is the voice of faith — not guessing, but relying on God's covenant. Abraham has learned, through decades of testing, that God guides those who act in obedience to his word."

Calvin: Abraham would not suffer his son to take a Canaanite wife — not from ethnic pride but from theological principle. The covenant line must not be absorbed into the peoples whose land God had promised. The separation is covenant fidelity.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on "put your hand under my thigh" explains this as an oath formula associated with the seat of generative power. The note observes that the gesture appears only twice in Genesis (here and 47:29) and survives in no other ancient Near Eastern source, making its full meaning uncertain.

The NET note on the thigh-oath and divine title 'LORD God of heaven and earth' — the most comprehensive designation of divine authority in Genesis — observes their combination makes this the most solemn oath formula in the patriarchal narratives, appropriate to the most comprehensive mission.

sections.header: Verses 10–27 — The Servant Prays; Rebekah Appears

poi.name: Spring outside the city (Harran area)
poi.text: The servant stops at the well outside the city in the evening — the standard meeting point for travellers and residents. His prayer is answered before it is finished: Rebekah appears at the exact moment his petition ends. The ordinary geography of a watering point becomes the site of providential encounter.

notes.note: MacArthur: The servant's prayer is a model of specific, faith-filled intercession. He asks for a very specific sign -- the woman who offers water to both him and his camels -- and receives it immediately (v.15-17). The prayer aligns the servant's request with the covenant purpose (finding a wife for Isaac) and trusts God to show his covenant faithfulness.

MacArthur: 'Praise be to the LORD, the God of my master Abraham, who has not abandoned his kindness and faithfulness to my master. As for me, the LORD has led me on the journey to the house of my master's relatives.' The servant's doxology is the narrative's theological comment: the entire chain of events -- prayer, sign, meeting, response -- is the LORD's guidance.

heb.paragraph: "The LORD... has not abandoned his kindness and faithfulness (ḥasdô weʾămittô) to my master" (v.27) — the servant's exclamation upon meeting Rebekah combines ḥesed ("covenant love") and ʾemet ("faithfulness/truth") in one of the OT's great theological compounds. The pairing ḥesed weʾĕmet appears in the revelation of God's name at Sinai (Exod 34:6: "abounding in love and faithfulness") and throughout the Psalms as the signature of divine character. Providence experienced in covenant terms is always both loving and faithful.

The servant had prayed: "let the girl to whom I say, 'Please let down your jar that I may have a drink,' and who says, 'Drink, and I'll water your camels too' — let her be the one you have chosen" (v.14). The sign-prayer is answered before it is finished (v.15: "before he had finished praying, Rebekah came out"). The "eyes" of covenant discernment — seeing what God is doing through what appears coincidental — are the servant's great gift. His testimony to Rebekah's family (vv.34-49) is one of Scripture's most elegant accounts of providential guidance narrated in faith.

The account of the servant's mission turns on three "meetings" — the servant meets Rebekah at the well, the servant meets Rebekah's family, and Rebekah will meet Isaac (v.65). The verb pāgaʿ ("to encounter/fall upon") is used of providential meetings — Elijah and the angel (1 Kgs 19:5), Jacob and the angels (32:1). The entire chapter is a theology of providential encounter: the servant prays; God arranges; the right person appears. The meeting at the well is not coincidence but the answer to covenant prayer.

hist.context: The literary technique of Genesis 24 is deliberate repetition. The servant's journey is described (vv.10–21), then narrated again in his speech to Laban (vv.34–49) with small but significant variations. This double narration slows the reader, creates suspense, and confirms the providential interpretation. In the servant's retelling, what appeared coincidental in the first account now appears as divinely orchestrated. Providence is often only visible in retrospect.

refs.note: Jacob's meeting with Rachel at the well in chapter 29 follows the same type-scene: journey to a well, encounter with a woman, drawing water, hospitality, betrothal. The pattern establishes a literary convention.
The careful matchmaking by Abraham's servant parallels Naomi's orchestration of Ruth's encounter with Boaz. Both narratives celebrate providence working through human initiative in marriage.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The servant's prayer at the well is the first explicitly prayer-of-guidance narrative in the Bible. Sarna: "The servant prays for a sign — not a miraculous sign but a providential one: that the girl who offers water to him and his camels unprompted will be God's choice. The test is designed to reveal character (hospitality, generosity, willingness to serve) rather than beauty or status."

The servant bows in worship immediately upon the sign being fulfilled: "the man bowed down and worshipped the LORD, saying 'Praise be to the LORD, the God of my master Abraham, who has not abandoned his kindness and faithfulness to my master.'" Sarna: "Worship precedes any further action. The servant's first response to answered prayer is thanksgiving, not congratulation to himself. This is the model of covenant gratitude."

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Rebekah draws water for the servant and then, "quickly," offers to water his camels. Alter: "The speed of her response is narratively significant: the text slows to describe Rebekah drawing water 'quickly' (vv.16,18,20,28,46) — the word appears five times in rapid succession. Her generosity is not reluctant or calculated; it is immediate and abundant. Ten camels drink a great deal of water."

Rebekah's response is marked by three speed indicators: 'quickly' (v.18), 'quickly' again (v.20), 'ran' (v.20). Alter notes the repeated hurrying characterises her as action-oriented and generous beyond the minimum required. She offers to water all ten camels — a significant physical commitment.

The servant's prayer models legitimate petition for divine guidance. Calvin: "He does not rashly prescribe to God what sign he should use, but simply proposes what would be a likely and reasonable indication of a virtuous wife. He commits the matter to God, and prays for clear guidance. This is not tempting God but humbly seeking divine confirmation for a covenant-critical decision."

Calvin: The servant sought a sign not from distrust but from faith — he believed God could provide and asked for a specific sign to recognise the provision. The prayer is not a test of God but a request for clarity about how to recognise God's acting.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on the sign of the water notes that the test is entirely character-based, not appearance-based. The note observes that Rebekah passes the test before the servant knows whose daughter she is — the character check precedes the genealogical check. This order is significant: providential guidance operates through character, not merely through family connection.

The NET note on Rebekah's description — 'very beautiful, a virgin, no man had known her' — observes that all three descriptors together establish her suitability as a covenant wife. The note traces the language against ancient Near Eastern marriage law.

sections.header: Verses 28–67 — Rebekah's Family; the Return; Marriage to Isaac

poi.name: Canaan / Negev (return journey)
poi.coords: Upper Mesopotamia back to southern Canaan
poi.text: The servant's return traverses the full route from Aram back to Canaan — the same route Abram first travelled. Isaac is at Beer Lahai Roi in the Negev when Rebekah arrives: the covenant son is at Hagar's well, the place named for God's sight, when he first sees his covenant wife.

notes.note: MacArthur: 'Will you go with this man?' 'I will go.' Rebekah's single-word decision mirrors Abraham's response to the call of God (12:1-4). Both left family and homeland for an unknown destination in response to a word from the LORD. The covenant pattern of radical obedient departure marks each generation's entry into the covenant inheritance.

MacArthur: Isaac took Rebekah into his mother Sarah's tent, and she became his wife. He loved her. The simple, direct statement of covenant love is significant: Isaac's love for Rebekah is the first explicit statement of marital love in Scripture. The covenant is passed through a love that has its ground in God's covenant faithfulness, not romantic circumstance.

heb.paragraph: Rebekah's family — Laban her brother and Bethuel her father (v.50), with her mother separately consulted (vv.53-58) — demonstrate the complex social structures of covenant negotiation. Laban's prominent role alongside Bethuel suggests he has significant household authority — a role that will characterise him in the Jacob narratives (ch.29-31). The mother's separate voice in the negotiation (v.55: "Let the young woman remain with us ten days or so") reflects that women's kinship networks have real, if informal, power in covenant family decisions.

The covenant negotiation climaxes in the family's consent: "Take her and go, and let her become the wife of your master's son, as the LORD has directed" (v.51). The consent formula reflects their recognition of divine direction (YHWH yāṣāʾ haddābār — "the matter has gone out from the LORD"). Even outside the formal covenant community, the family of Nahor recognises that the servant's mission has divine mandate. The consent is not merely social permission but theological recognition of providential direction.

"Isaac... married Rebekah. So she became his wife, and he loved her; and Isaac was comforted after his mother's death" (v.67). The verb nāḥam — the root of Noah's name ("he will comfort us") — appears here as the emotional resolution of the chapter and of the grief implied since Sarah's death in ch.23. The marriage covenant is the personal embodiment of the covenant promise: the line will continue, the comfort comes in the form of a wife prepared by divine providence, and the covenant's next generation begins in love.

hist.context: Rebekah's two-word answer — "I will go" — parallels Abraham's departure from Ur in its radical willingness to leave everything and go to an unknown land to a man she has never seen. Her family blesses her with words that echo the Abrahamic promise: "May you increase to thousands upon thousands; may your offspring possess the gate of their enemies" (v.60). She enters the story already carrying the covenant blessing.

refs.note: Ruth's "where you go I will go" parallels Rebekah's "I will go" — both are covenant declarations of loyalty from a woman leaving her homeland to join the covenant people.
Rebekah's "I will go" echoes Abraham's obedience to "go" — the covenant consistently calls people to radical, immediate departure toward the promise.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Laban and Bethuel's response — "this is from the LORD; we can say nothing to you one way or the other" — is a remarkable acknowledgment of divine providence by the Mesopotamian family. Sarna: "The response is a covenant formula — the recognition that the hand of God has arranged the marriage. The family of Rebekah recognises what Abraham's servant has demonstrated: the divine will has been made clear through the events of the day."

Isaac brings Rebekah into his mother's tent; she becomes his wife; "he loved her." Sarna: "The narrative sequence — marriage before love — reflects the ancient world's understanding of marriage as a social institution that creates love, not merely an expression of pre-existing love. The statement 'he loved her' is the chapter's emotional resolution: the mission accomplished, the covenant continued, the family bond established."

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Isaac goes out to meditate in the field at evening; he looks up and sees the camels approaching; Rebekah looks up and sees Isaac. Alter: "The mutual looking establishes the connection before any words are spoken. The silent recognition — each seeing the other for the first time — is one of the most romantically precise moments in the entire patriarchal narrative."

Rebekah's one-word decision — elekh ('I will go') — is the narrative's hinge. Alter notes she makes the same unconditional commitment Abraham made in 12:1: leaving family, homeland, and certainty for an unknown destination. The covenant pattern recurs in a new generation.

Calvin on Isaac's love for Rebekah: "Moses notes that Isaac was so wedded to his wife that he forgot the grief he had borne for his mother. Marriage is the sovereign remedy for loneliness and grief. And the love is noted — not lust or possessiveness, but genuine affection. This is the creation pattern (2:24) working itself out in covenant history."

Calvin: Moses does not say Isaac loved Rebekah at first sight but that after she became his wife he loved her. Love is the fruit of the marriage, not its cause. Commitment precedes feeling, and feeling deepens from within commitment.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on "Isaac brought her into the tent of his mother Sarah" observes this is a symbolically loaded act: the next matriarch is installed in the previous matriarch's tent. The note observes that the maternal tent is the center of domestic life in nomadic culture. Isaac's grief for his mother (noted in the verse) is resolved through the establishment of the new family unit.

The NET note on Isaac 'going out to meditate' (lasuach) discusses the interpretive options and concludes some form of prayerful walking is the most probable meaning. The evening setting has a liminal quality appropriate to the moment of meeting.

ppl.name: The Servant (likely Eliezer)
ppl.role: Abraham's chief servant; model of faithful stewardship
ppl.text: Prays before acting, credits God immediately, refuses to eat before completing his report, does not deviate from his mission. A model of servant leadership — completely self-effacing, entirely focused on his master's purpose. His name is never given in this chapter.

ppl.name: Rebekah
ppl.text: Identified by extraordinary generosity before her identity is known. Her "I will go" is one of the most consequential two-word decisions in the entire Bible. She becomes the mother of Jacob and Esau, the next generation of covenant carriers.

ppl.name: Isaac
ppl.text: Largely passive in this chapter — the subject of everyone's efforts. His role is to receive. He is "comforted after his mother's death" — the grief of ch.23 finds healing in ch.24. Marriage becomes a vehicle of divine comfort.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"Isaac brought her into the tent of his mother Sarah, and he married Rebekah. So she became his wife, and he loved her; and Isaac was comforted after his mother's death."ESV"Then Isaac brought her into the tent of Sarah his mother and took Rebekah, and she became his wife, and he loved her. So Isaac was comforted after his mother's death."KJV"And Isaac brought her into his mother Sarah's tent, and took Rebekah, and she became his wife; and he loved her: and Isaac was comforted after his mother's death."

src.title: ANE Marriage Customs
src.quote: Marriage negotiation in the ancient world involved family representatives, bride price, and parental consent. Genesis 24 follows this pattern while uniquely emphasizing the bride's own consent (v.58 — "Will you go?"). This element of Rebekah's choice is notable in an ancient context.

rec.text: Commentators from Augustine to Chrysostom to Calvin have used Genesis 24 as the primary biblical text on divine providence in ordinary life. No angel appears, no miracle occurs — just a prayer answered by the first young woman at a well. God's guidance operates through natural means and human character.

Patristic interpreters read the chapter allegorically: Abraham = God the Father; Isaac = Christ; the servant = the Holy Spirit; Rebekah = the Church called from the nations to be Christ's bride. The allegorical reading, while not controlling, captures genuine theological resonances.

rows.label: The Willingness to Go: Covenant Pattern in a New Generation
rows.text: Will you go with this man? I will go. The one-word answer is the narrative's hinge. Rebekah makes the same unconditional commitment Abraham made in 12:1 -- leaving everything for an unknown destination.

rows.label: The Meeting at Evening: Meditation and Movement
rows.text: Isaac meditates in the field at evening; he lifts his eyes and sees the camels coming. Rebekah lifts her eyes and sees Isaac. The mutual seeing at the threshold of the meeting is the patriarchal narrative's most tender encounter.

hebtext.note: Abraham "sent his servant" (šālaḥ, v.2) to find a wife for Isaac — the same verb used of God "sending" his covenant agents throughout Scripture. The servant is unnamed (possibly Eliezer of Damascus, mentioned in 15:2) but given a solemn oath-commission. The entire chapter is the longest narrative in Genesis, with the servant's story told twice — once as it happened, once as he recounts it. The doubling is theologically significant: covenant mission requires testimony, and testimony requires witness.

"Put your hand under my thigh" (v.2) — the oath-gesture of placing the hand under the patriarch's thigh (near the circumcision/covenant-organ) signals the solemnity of the commission. The same gesture appears at Jacob's deathbed (47:29). The thigh-oath invokes the covenant sign of circumcision: the servant is bound by the covenant itself, not merely by social obligation. Breaking this oath would be a breach of covenant-oath — one of the most serious offences in the ancient Near East.

"He will send his angel before you" (v.7) — Abraham's confidence that God will send a malʾāk to guide the servant echoes the angel who guided Hagar (16:7) and will guide Israel in the wilderness (Exod 23:20). The conviction that divine guidance accompanies covenant mission is not optimism but theology: the covenant God is the one who sends, sustains, and guides those sent on his behalf. The servant's subsequent prayer (vv.12-14) and its immediate answer (vv.15-27) confirm that the angel's guidance is real and specific.

"The LORD... has not abandoned his kindness and faithfulness (ḥasdô weʾămittô) to my master" (v.27) — the servant's exclamation upon meeting Rebekah combines ḥesed ("covenant love") and ʾemet ("faithfulness/truth") in one of the OT's great theological compounds. The pairing ḥesed weʾĕmet appears in the revelation of God's name at Sinai (Exod 34:6: "abounding in love and faithfulness") and throughout the Psalms as the signature of divine character. Providence experienced in covenant terms is always both loving and faithful.

The servant had prayed: "let the girl to whom I say, 'Please let down your jar that I may have a drink,' and who says, 'Drink, and I'll water your camels too' — let her be the one you have chosen" (v.14). The "eyes" of covenant discernment — seeing what God is doing through what appears coincidental — are the servant's great gift. His testimony to Rebekah's family (vv.34-49) is one of Scripture's most elegant accounts of providential guidance narrated in faith.

The account of the servant's mission turns on three "meetings" — the servant meets Rebekah at the well, the servant meets Rebekah's family, and Rebekah will meet Isaac (v.65). The verb pāgaʿ ("to encounter/fall upon") is used of providential meetings — Elijah and the angel (1 Kgs 19:5), Jacob and the angels (32:1). The entire chapter is a theology of providential encounter: the servant prays; God arranges; the right person appears. The meeting at the well is not coincidence but the answer to covenant prayer.

Rebekah's family — Laban her brother and Bethuel her father (v.50), with her mother separately consulted (vv.53-58) — demonstrate the complex social structures of covenant negotiation. Laban's prominent role alongside Bethuel suggests he has significant household authority — a role that will characterise him in the Jacob narratives (ch.29-31). The mother's separate voice in the negotiation (v.55: "Let the young woman remain with us ten days or so") reflects that women's kinship networks have real, if informal, power in covenant family decisions.

The covenant negotiation climaxes in the family's consent: "Take her and go, and let her become the wife of your master's son, as the LORD has directed" (v.51). The consent formula reflects their recognition of divine direction (YHWH yāṣāʾ haddābār — "the matter has gone out from the LORD"). Even outside the formal covenant community, the family of Nahor recognises that the servant's mission has divine mandate. The consent is not merely social permission but theological recognition of providential direction.

"Isaac... married Rebekah. So she became his wife, and he loved her; and Isaac was comforted after his mother's death" (v.67). The verb nāḥam — the root of Noah's name ("he will comfort us") — appears here as the emotional resolution of the chapter and of the grief implied since Sarah's death in ch.23. tx.title: Aram Naharaim — geographic identification
tx.content: MT reads 'Aram Naharaim' (Aram of the two rivers). The LXX translates as Μεσοποταμίαν (Mesopotamia), which became the standard geographic term in Greek and later European languages. The SP follows MT. Targum Onqelos renders it as 'Aram on the Euphrates.'
tx.note: The LXX's Mesopotamia translation is one of its most successful neologisms — a Greek coinage that permanently shaped Western geography.

tx.title: Oath ceremony — 'put your hand under my thigh'
tx.content: MT's euphemistic 'under my thigh' (taḥat yĕrēkî) is rendered literally in the LXX (ὑπὸ τὸν μηρόν μου). Targum Onqelos substitutes 'upon the sign of my circumcision,' making the covenantal nature explicit. The SP follows MT without modification.
tx.note: The Targum's rendering is interpretive but plausible — the oath invokes the covenant sign. This is one of the clearest examples of Onqelos explaining rather than translating.

debate.title: The Servant's Prayer: Model for Providential Guidance?

positions.name: Specific sign-seeking (questionable)
positions.argument: The servant sets up a specific test (the girl who offers water to the camels). This is analogous to "fleece" requests (Judg 6:36-40). Such specific sign-seeking may reflect limited faith rather than mature trust.

positions.name: Legitimate prayer for wisdom
positions.argument: The servant prays in complete dependence, gives God complete freedom to act, and responds with immediate worship when God answers. The prayer is a model of humble, specific, grateful intercession — not manipulative sign-seeking.

coaching.tip: The servant's prayer is remarkably specific: the right woman will not only give him water but volunteer to water ten camels. A thirsty camel drinks 25 gallons. This is a request for extravagant, unsolicited generosity — the servant is testing for character, not beauty.

Rebekah is asked 'Will you go with this man?' and answers 'I will go' — the same decisive language God used with Abraham in chapter 12. She leaves family and homeland on faith. The narrative frames her as a true counterpart to Abraham.

prayer_prompt: As you reflect on Abraham's servant praying for specific guidance at the well, consider asking God to show you how to bring your practical decisions — not just your crises — before Him with the same specificity and expectation.
